overview.brief_description: Palma is the vibrant capital of the Balearic island of Mallorca, known for its stunning Gothic architecture, lively waterfront, and Mediterranean charm. This historic city seamlessly blends rich cultural heritage with modern amenities, making it a top destination for travelers seeking sun, sea, and culture.
why_visit: Explore the magnificent Palma Cathedral with its breathtaking Gothic design
Stroll along the picturesque old town with its narrow streets and lively plazas
Relax on beautiful nearby beaches like Cala Major and Playa de Palma
Enjoy vibrant nightlife and exquisite Mediterranean cuisine
overview.best_time_to_visit: May to October, when the weather is warm, sunny, and perfect for beach and outdoor activities

seasonal_notes.spring: Mild temperatures and blooming landscapes make it ideal for sightseeing and outdoor exploration.
seasonal_notes.summer: Hot and sunny, perfect for beach lovers but also the busiest tourist season.
seasonal_notes.autumn: Pleasant weather with fewer crowds, great for cultural visits and hiking.
seasonal_notes.winter: Cool and quiet, offering a peaceful experience and lower prices, though beach activities are limited.

practical_info.visit_duration: 3 to 5 days recommended to fully experience the city and surroundings
practical_info.getting_around: Buses, taxis, rental bikes, and car rentals are common; the city is walkable especially in the old town
practical_info.safety: Generally safe for travelers, usual precautions for petty theft in tourist areas advised

must_do: Visit Palma Cathedral (La Seu) and admire its stunning architecture
Walk through the historic Old Town and Plaza Mayor
Relax at Playa de Palma or Cala Major beaches
Explore the Almudaina Palace and its beautiful gardens
Enjoy local cuisine at Mercado de l'Olivar
worth_doing: Take a boat trip along the coastline
Visit Es Baluard Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art
Climb Bellver Castle for panoramic city views
Shop in the boutiques along Passeig del Born
hidden_gems: Explore the quaint neighborhood of Santa Catalina with its trendy cafes
Visit the lesser-known Convent of Santa Clara
Wander through the colorful street art in the La Lonja district
Relax in the lesser-visited Parc de la Mar
Check out the artisan markets on weekends

sites.name: La Seu Cathedral
sites.description: Palma's iconic Gothic cathedral, officially known as Catedral-Basílica de Santa María de Mallorca, boasts stunning stained-glass windows and a soaring interior redesigned by Gaudí. Completed in the early 17th century, it overlooks the sea, offering breathtaking views and a deep dive into Mallorca's religious art and architecture.
sites.booking_tips: Book tickets online to skip queues.

sites.name: Palau de l'Almudaina
sites.description: A magnificent Moorish royal palace adjacent to La Seu Cathedral, Palau de l'Almudaina blends Islamic and Gothic architectural styles. Once the residence of Mallorcan monarchs, it now serves as a ceremonial palace and museum with impressive tapestries and medieval artifacts.
sites.booking_tips: Combined ticket available with cathedral visits.

sites.name: Es Baluard Museu d'Art Modern i Contemporani
sites.description: This modern art museum located in a former fortress offers an excellent collection of contemporary Mallorcan and international artists. The building itself features impressive viewpoints overlooking Palma Bay and combines historic walls with modern architecture.
sites.booking_tips: Tickets available online; free on Sundays after 15:00

sites.name: Parc de la Mar
sites.description: Situated below the cathedral, Parc de la Mar is a lovely seaside park with large reflecting pools and sculptures, perfect for a leisurely stroll. It offers spectacular views of La Seu Cathedral and the Mediterranean, making it a favorite spot for locals and visitors to relax and people-watch.
sites.seasonal_notes: Pleasant year-round, ideal in spring and fall

sites.name: Mercat de l'Olivar
sites.description: Palma's largest and liveliest market, Mercat de l'Olivar offers fresh seafood, produce, meats, and local delicacies. The market's vibrant atmosphere and quality stalls make it a perfect spot for food lovers to sample Mallorcan specialties and mingle with locals.
sites.booking_tips: Arrive early for the freshest products

sites.name: Castell de Bellver
sites.description: A unique circular Gothic castle perched on a hill overlooking Palma, Castell de Bellver dates back to the 14th century. It offers panoramic views of the city and harbor, a museum of Mallorcan history, and extensive wooded parkland surrounding the fortress.
sites.seasonal_notes: Spring and autumn ideal for hiking the surrounding park
sites.booking_tips: Buy combined ticket for castle and museum

sites.name: Passeig des Born
sites.description: The elegant tree-lined boulevard at Palma’s historic center, Passeig des Born is famous for its upscale shops, cafes, and vibrant atmosphere. This 19th-century promenade is perfect for a leisurely stroll and people-watching while soaking in the city’s charm.
sites.seasonal_notes: Bustling year-round, lively especially in spring and fall

sites.name: Fundació Pilar i Joan Miró
sites.description: Dedicated to the works of Joan Miró, this foundation showcases a rich collection of paintings, sculptures, and ceramics. The artist’s studio and gardens provide insight into his creative process amidst a serene setting on the outskirts of Palma.
sites.booking_tips: Purchase tickets online to ensure entry.

sites.name: Es Jonquet
sites.description: A picturesque traditional fishermen’s quarter with narrow winding streets and whitewashed houses, Es Jonquet offers a glimpse into Palma’s maritime past. Quaint restaurants and a laid-back atmosphere make it a charming area to explore on foot.

visit_profile.weather_fallback: Visit nearby indoor cafes or bars

sites.name: Plaça Major
sites.description: Palma’s main square is a lively hub framed by arcaded buildings hosting shops, cafes, and occasional street markets. Its vibrant atmosphere and central location make it a perfect meeting point and place to enjoy local life.

sites.name: Basilica de Sant Francesc
sites.description: This 13th-century Gothic church, dedicated to Saint Francis, features a peaceful cloister and a Baroque altar. Located near the Old Town, it provides a quiet retreat from the bustling city with its serene gardens and intricate architecture.

sites.name: Jardí del Bisbe
sites.description: A hidden garden near the cathedral, Jardí del Bisbe offers lush greenery, fountains, and peaceful benches surrounded by historic walls. This tranquil spot is ideal for a quiet break while exploring Palma’s Old Town.
sites.seasonal_notes: Beautiful in spring when flowers bloom

sites.name: Museu Fundación Juan March
sites.description: This museum offers a fine collection of 20th-century Spanish art including works by Picasso, Dalí, and Miró. Its modern building and rotating exhibitions make it a cultural highlight in Palma’s art scene.
sites.booking_tips: Check the website for current exhibitions and opening hours.

sites.name: Cala Major Beach
sites.description: Just a short drive from Palma’s center, Cala Major is a sandy beach with clear turquoise waters, ideal for swimming and sunbathing. The beach is lined with restaurants and cafes, making it a popular spot to unwind after sightseeing.
sites.seasonal_notes: Best visited in late spring through early autumn

visit_profile.weather_fallback: Visit nearby cafes or indoor pools

sites.name: La Lonja
sites.description: A fine example of Gothic civil architecture, La Lonja was originally built as a merchants’ exchange in the 15th century and now hosts art exhibitions and cultural events. Its elegant halls and cloister-like courtyard provide a glimpse into Palma’s maritime trading history.

sites.name: Molinar Beach
sites.description: A quieter alternative to Palma’s central beaches, Molinar offers a sandy shore and calm waters favored by locals. The adjacent promenade is dotted with traditional seafood restaurants and is perfect for a relaxed seaside walk.

sites.name: Convento de Santa Clara
sites.description: Located near the port, this former convent dates back to the 14th century and features a beautiful cloister and chapel. Its peaceful gardens and historic architecture offer insight into Palma’s religious life across centuries.

sites.name: Passeig Marítim
sites.description: This bustling waterfront promenade stretches along Palma’s harbor, lined with palm trees, yachts, and bars. It’s perfect for sunset walks with spectacular views of the bay and the city skyline.
sites.seasonal_notes: Very pleasant spring through autumn evenings

neighborhoods.name: Ciutat Antiga
neighborhoods.character: The historic heart of Palma, bursting with centuries-old architecture and narrow winding streets. It offers an enchanting blend of medieval charm and lively urban life.

location.description: Situated at the core of Palma, encompassing the original city walls area.

history.overview: Founded by the Romans and later shaped by Moorish and Catalan influences, Ciutat Antiga is Palma’s oldest district.
history.significance: It represents Palma’s cultural and historical roots, preserving Gothic and Renaissance architecture.
notable_events: Construction of Palma Cathedral in the 13th century

practical_info.safety: Generally safe but watch for pickpockets in crowded tourist spots
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Spring and autumn for pleasant weather and fewer crowds
practical_info.walkability: Highly walkable with pedestrian-only streets and plazas

attractions.name: Palma Cathedral (La Seu)
attractions.description: A stunning Gothic cathedral overlooking the sea with magnificent stained glass windows.
attractions.appeal: Iconic landmark and architectural marvel of Palma.

attractions.name: Arab Baths
attractions.description: One of the few remnants of Moorish Palma, showcasing ancient bathhouse ruins.
attractions.appeal: A glimpse into Palma’s Islamic past.

dining.name: Forn de Sant Joan
dining.known_for: Creative local dishes and elegant ambiance

insider_tips: Visit early morning to avoid crowds at Palma Cathedral.
Explore the hidden courtyards and small artisan shops around Plaça Major.
Try local pastries in small cafes tucked away in side streets.

neighborhoods.name: Santa Catalina
neighborhoods.character: A vibrant, bohemian neighborhood famous for its bustling market and eclectic dining scene. It’s Palma’s trendy hotspot with a youthful and creative vibe.

location.description: West of the Old Town, just a short walk from the city center.

history.overview: Originally a fishing village, Santa Catalina evolved into a lively artisan and market district.
history.significance: Known for its traditional market that remains a community hub despite gentrification.
notable_events: Transformation into Palma’s culinary district in the 21st century

practical_info.safety: Safe with a friendly, community feel
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Year-round, lively especially in evenings and weekends
practical_info.walkability: Very walkable with narrow streets and pedestrian zones

attractions.name: Santa Catalina Market
attractions.description: A bustling indoor market offering fresh produce, seafood, and local delicacies.
attractions.appeal: Ideal for tasting authentic Mallorcan flavors.

dining.name: La Bodeguilla
dining.known_for: Traditional Spanish tapas with a modern twist

insider_tips: Visit the market early for the freshest seafood and local produce.
Stay for dinner and enjoy the vibrant tapas bars after sunset.
Look for street art murals along the main streets for great photos.

neighborhoods.name: La Lonja
neighborhoods.character: A stylish and fashionable neighborhood centered around Palma’s old port area, blending maritime charm with upscale dining and nightlife.

location.description: Southwest of the Old Town, adjacent to the harbor.

history.overview: La Lonja was historically Palma’s shipping and trading hub, now revitalized as a trendy district.
history.significance: Its transformation showcases the city’s maritime heritage alongside modern leisure culture.

practical_info.safety: Safe and well-patrolled, popular with tourists
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Evenings in spring and summer for nightlife
practical_info.walkability: Highly walkable with scenic waterfront promenades

attractions.name: La Lonja Church
attractions.description: A beautiful example of Gothic architecture once used by merchants.
attractions.appeal: Historic site with serene ambiance near the harbor.

dining.name: Ombu
dining.known_for: Fresh fish dishes with Mediterranean flair

insider_tips: Walk along the marina at sunset for stunning views.
Reserve a table at seafood restaurants in advance during peak season.
Visit art galleries on quieter afternoons to meet local artists.

neighborhoods.name: Paseo Marítimo
neighborhoods.character: A long, lively waterfront boulevard popular for evening strolls, trendy clubs, and upscale hotels. It’s a hotspot for nightlife and leisure with sea breezes and palm trees.

location.description: Runs along Palma’s coastline, north and west of the Old Town.

history.overview: Once a simple coastal road, it has become Palma’s premier nightlife and hotel district.
history.significance: It symbolizes Palma’s modern leisure culture and seaside appeal.
notable_events: Growth of beach clubs and nightlife venues

transit: Bus line stops along the promenade
practical_info.safety: Generally safe but busy at night—stay alert
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Summer evenings for nightlife and events
practical_info.walkability: Very walkable with wide sidewalks and bike lanes

attractions.name: Bellver Castle
attractions.description: Circular castle perched on a hill with panoramic views of Palma and the sea.
attractions.appeal: Historic landmark close to the promenade offering hiking and photo ops.

insider_tips: Visit Bellver Castle in the late afternoon to avoid midday heat.
Check local listings for summer beach parties and music events.
Use the bike lanes to explore the full length of the promenade.

neighborhoods.name: Son Armadams
neighborhoods.character: A quiet, leafy residential neighborhood favored by locals, with elegant villas and a calm atmosphere. It offers a peaceful retreat from the busy city center.

location.description: Northwest of the Old Town, adjacent to Santa Catalina.

history.overview: Traditionally a suburban district with aristocratic estates, now a sought-after residential area.
history.significance: Known for its green spaces and quiet streets within close reach of Palma’s amenities.
notable_events: Development of garden estates in early 20th century

transit: Bus stops connecting to city center
practical_info.walkability: Walkable but more residential with fewer commercial spots

attractions.name: Jardines de S'Hort del Rei
attractions.description: Peaceful gardens offering shaded paths and Mediterranean flora.
attractions.appeal: Perfect spot for relaxation and nature walks.

dining.name: Bar Espana
dining.known_for: Authentic Mallorcan tapas in a local setting

insider_tips: Enjoy an early morning walk through the Jardines de S'Hort del Rei.
Visit local bakeries for traditional Mallorcan pastries in the neighborhood.
Avoid weekends if you prefer quiet streets, as some locals take short trips into the city center.

neighborhoods.name: La Rambla
neighborhoods.character: A lively boulevard and neighborhood known for its shopping, street performances, and vibrant daytime atmosphere. It’s a bustling corridor connecting the Old Town to the modern parts of Palma.

location.description: Extends south from Plaça d’Espanya towards the seafront.

history.overview: Developed as a main thoroughfare in the 19th century, La Rambla has been Palma’s commercial artery.
history.significance: It serves as a cultural and social meeting point with a mix of old and new Palma.

transit: Plaça d’Espanya metro and bus station
practical_info.walkability: Highly walkable with wide pedestrian areas

attractions.name: Plaça d’Espanya
attractions.description: A grand square serving as Palma’s transport hub and gateway to the city.

insider_tips: Visit local artisan stalls on market days for unique souvenirs.
Try the traditional almond ice cream at Ca’n Joan de S’aigo.
Stay alert for street performers and impromptu music shows along the boulevard.

fine_dining.name: Adrián Quetglas
fine_dining.description: Innovative cuisine blending Mediterranean and international flavors, led by Chef Adrián Quetglas, awarded a Michelin star.
fine_dining.atmosphere: Elegant and intimate with contemporary decor
fine_dining.local_tips: Try the tasting menu with paired wines for the full experience.

fine_dining.name: Marc Fosh
fine_dining.description: Michelin-starred restaurant offering creative dishes using local Mallorcan ingredients with a refined touch.
fine_dining.local_tips: Book well in advance, especially on weekends.

fine_dining.name: DINS Santi Taura
fine_dining.description: Chef Santi Taura’s innovative take on traditional Mallorcan cuisine, awarded a Michelin star.
fine_dining.atmosphere: Chic yet cozy with minimalist design
fine_dining.local_tips: Ask for the chef’s tasting menu to explore Mallorcan flavors.

fine_dining.name: Toque
fine_dining.description: Small, Michelin-starred restaurant offering a seasonal tasting menu with Mediterranean influences.
fine_dining.local_tips: Perfect for adventurous diners keen on tasting menus.

casual_dining.name: Ca'n Joan de s'Aigo
casual_dining.description: Historic establishment known for classic Mallorcan dishes and famous ensaimadas and hot chocolate.
casual_dining.local_tips: Try the ensaimada fresh from the oven.

casual_dining.name: Quina Creu
casual_dining.description: Vibrant tapas bar serving creative small plates with local ingredients in a lively setting.
casual_dining.local_tips: Reserve ahead for weekends and try the octopus tapas.

casual_dining.name: Naan Project
casual_dining.description: Creative Mediterranean dishes in a relaxed atmosphere with a focus on fresh, local produce.
casual_dining.local_tips: Try the house-made bread and seasonal salads.

casual_dining.name: Bar España
casual_dining.description: Classic tapas bar with a long local tradition, perfect for a casual evening sampling various small dishes.
casual_dining.local_tips: Arrive early to get a seat during busy hours.

casual_dining.name: La Rosa Vermutería
casual_dining.description: Trendy spot specializing in vermouth and traditional tapas with a modern twist.
casual_dining.local_tips: Pair your vermouth with the anchovy tapas.

casual_dining.name: S'Angel
casual_dining.description: Casual dining with a focus on traditional Mallorcan flavors and fresh seafood.
casual_dining.local_tips: Try the tumbet, a Mallorcan vegetable dish.

street_food.name: Mercat de l'Olivar
street_food.description: Palma’s main food market offering fresh seafood, tapas, and local produce to enjoy on the spot or take away.
street_food.local_tips: Try the fresh oysters or pa amb oli from market stalls.

street_food.name: Mercat de Santa Catalina
street_food.description: Trendy market with a mix of traditional and modern street food stalls, perfect for a casual meal or snack.
street_food.local_tips: Try the local cheeses and freshly squeezed juices.

street_food.name: Pa amb Oli Street Vendors
street_food.description: Various street vendors offering pa amb oli, Mallorca’s signature bread with olive oil, tomatoes, cheese, and ham.
street_food.local_tips: Best enjoyed with local sobrassada or Mahón cheese.

street_food.name: Chiringuito Cala Major
street_food.description: Casual beach bars offering grilled seafood and tapas ideal for enjoying a relaxed meal by the sea.
street_food.local_tips: Try the grilled sardines and refreshing local white wine.

bars.name: Bar Abaco
bars.description: Opulent cocktail bar set in a historic mansion with extravagant floral decorations and creative drinks.
bars.local_tips: Try their signature cocktails and enjoy the baroque interiors.

bars.name: Brondo Architect Hotel Rooftop Bar
bars.description: Trendy rooftop bar with panoramic views of Palma, offering creative cocktails and a relaxed vibe.
bars.local_tips: Visit for sunset to catch spectacular views of the cathedral.

bars.name: Ginbo
bars.description: Specialized gin bar with a vast selection of gins and tailor-made cocktails in an intimate setting.
bars.local_tips: Ask the bartenders for personalized gin recommendations.

bars.name: La Rosa Vermutería
bars.description: Popular bar known for a wide variety of vermouths and traditional tapas.
bars.local_tips: Great spot for an afternoon vermouth with anchovies.

cafes.name: Forn Fondo
cafes.description: Traditional Mallorcan bakery and café famous for ensaimadas and pastries.
cafes.local_tips: Try the freshly baked ensaimadas with your coffee.

cafes.name: Rosevelvet Bakery
cafes.description: Cozy café offering excellent coffee, homemade cakes, and healthy brunch options.
cafes.local_tips: Their carrot cake is a local favorite.

cafes.name: Café Riutort
cafes.description: Renowned specialty coffee roaster and café with expertly brewed espresso and filter coffee.
cafes.local_tips: Try a single-origin pour-over for a true coffee experience.

cafes.name: Espla Café
cafes.description: Small café focusing on high-quality coffee and fresh, local ingredients for brunch dishes.
cafes.local_tips: Their avocado toast pairs perfectly with a flat white.

food_experiences.name: Palma Food Walking Tour
food_experiences.description: Guided walking tours exploring traditional Mallorcan cuisine, tapas bars, and local markets.
food_experiences.local_tips: Book morning tours to visit the markets at their freshest.

food_experiences.name: Mallorcan Cooking Class with Local Chef
food_experiences.description: Hands-on cooking classes teaching traditional Mallorcan dishes like tumbet and sobrasada preparation.
food_experiences.local_tips: Classes often include a market visit to select fresh ingredients.

food_experiences.name: Mercat de l'Olivar Tasting Experience
food_experiences.description: Self-guided or group tasting experience inside Palma’s main market sampling fresh seafood, cheeses, and wines.
food_experiences.local_tips: Try the fresh oysters and local wines at the market stalls.

food_experiences.name: Wine and Cheese Tasting Tour
food_experiences.description: Guided tastings focusing on Mallorcan wines paired with local cheeses and cured meats.
food_experiences.local_tips: Combine with a visit to a nearby vineyard for a full-day experience.

food_experiences.name: Paella Cooking Workshop
food_experiences.description: Interactive workshop teaching how to prepare authentic Spanish paella using local Mallorcan ingredients.
food_experiences.local_tips: Book in advance, especially during summer months.

spring: Asparagus dishes with local olive oil
summer: Ensalada Mallorquina - a refreshing tomato and onion salad
Grilled sardines and other fresh seafood
winter: Caldo Mallorquín - traditional hearty vegetable and meat soup

transport_types.notes: Multiple daily flights from Palma de Mallorca Airport to Barcelona-El Prat; fastest and most popular connection.
Night and day ferries from Palma port to Barcelona port; scenic and convenient for vehicles.
Direct flights from Palma de Mallorca Airport to Madrid-Barajas; most efficient way.
Direct flights connecting Palma and Valencia; limited but regular service.
Weekly ferry service mainly for vehicles and freight; check schedules.
Direct flights to Paris Charles de Gaulle and Orly airports; best for international connections.
Direct flights to Rome Fiumicino and Ciampino airports; popular for leisure travelers.
Direct flights to Berlin Brandenburg Airport; no train or ferry connection.
Flights to Heathrow, Gatwick, and Stansted; most convenient way to reach London.
Direct flights to Lisbon Humberto Delgado Airport; no direct ferry or train.
Direct flights to Marseille Provence Airport; popular for southern France access.
Weekly ferry service; primarily used for vehicles and freight.
Direct flights to Malaga-Costa del Sol Airport; popular for beach tourism.

Spring.name: Fira de Primavera (Spring Fair)
Spring.description: A lively fair with local crafts, food stalls, and live music celebrating the arrival of spring in Palma.

Spring.name: Cycling along the Palma Bay Promenade
Spring.description: Enjoy mild spring weather by cycling the scenic seafront promenade with beautiful views of the Mediterranean.

Spring.name: Visit to Bellver Castle
Spring.description: Explore the unique circular Gothic castle and its extensive park, perfect for pleasant springtime walks.

Spring.name: Seasonal tasting of Sobrasada
Spring.description: Sample Mallorca’s traditional cured sausage, often enjoyed fresh in spring markets and tapas bars.

Summer.name: Palma International Boat Show
Summer.description: A prestigious event showcasing luxury yachts and marine technology, attracting enthusiasts worldwide.

Summer.name: Beach Day at Cala Major
Summer.description: Relax or swim at one of Palma’s most popular sandy beaches, ideal for sunbathing and water sports in hot weather.

Summer.name: Open-air concerts at Parc de la Mar
Summer.description: Enjoy live music performances set against the backdrop of Palma’s cathedral under summer skies.

Summer.name: Tasting Ensaimada Pastries
Summer.description: Try the iconic Mallorcan spiral pastry, best enjoyed fresh from local bakeries during summer mornings.

Summer.name: Sailing and Windsurfing
Summer.description: Take advantage of warm winds and calm seas for sailing and windsurfing lessons or rentals along Palma’s coast.

Autumn.name: Fira del Ram
Autumn.description: A traditional autumn fair with rides, artisan stalls, and local food celebrating Mallorcan culture.

Autumn.name: Hiking Serra de Tramuntana foothills
Autumn.description: Enjoy cooler autumn temperatures while hiking scenic trails with panoramic views near Palma.

Autumn.name: Wine tasting tours
Autumn.description: Explore Mallorca’s vineyards with tours and tastings of local reds and whites in the mild autumn weather.

Autumn.name: Visit to Palma Cathedral
Autumn.description: Discover the impressive Gothic architecture and seasonal exhibitions inside the iconic La Seu cathedral.

Autumn.name: Sampling roasted chestnuts and panellets
Autumn.description: Enjoy traditional autumn treats like roasted chestnuts and almond sweets at local markets and cafes.

Winter.name: Christmas Markets in Plaza Mayor
Winter.description: Experience festive stalls selling crafts, decorations, and seasonal treats in Palma’s main square.

Winter.name: Visit to Es Baluard Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art
Winter.description: Escape cooler weather by exploring cutting-edge art exhibitions in this waterfront museum.

Winter.name: Winter walks in Parc de la Mar
Winter.description: Mild winter temperatures make for pleasant strolls with views of the cathedral and bay.

Winter.name: Tasting Caldera de Llagosta
Winter.description: Try this traditional Mallorcan lobster stew, a warming winter specialty served in local restaurants.

Winter.name: Cycling in mild winter weather
Winter.description: Take advantage of Palma’s mild winters by cycling quieter city streets and nearby countryside routes.

ranges.notes: Cool mild weather with occasional rain; Epiphany celebrations bring local cultural events but also some crowds in Palma center.
Post-holiday period with cooler weather averaging 12°C; fewer tourists but some rain and wind; quieter city.
Cool and mild with increasing daylight; good for cultural sightseeing and off-season prices; some local events start to pick up.
Stable mild winter weather; ideal for quiet exploration and local museum visits; low tourist numbers.
Cool temperatures around 13°C; few tourists; some rain possible; good for budget travelers and quiet city walks.
Mild weather with festive Carnaval events attracting local and some international visitors; lively atmosphere but not overcrowded.
Post-Carnaval calm; cooler and quieter; excellent for cultural visits, museums, and coastal walks.
Weather begins to improve slightly; low crowds; good option for off-season budget travelers and cultural tourists.
Spring arrives with mild temperatures averaging 15°C; occasional rain; quiet city with nature beginning to bloom.

ranges.event: Semana Santa (Easter Week - variable dates, often late March/April)
ranges.notes: Early Semana Santa celebrations sometimes start in March; religious processions and cultural events increase crowds and atmosphere.
Warmer days up to 17-18°C; outdoor cafes and parks become pleasant; moderate tourist influx starting.
City livens with music, flower shows, and parades; good weather; increasing visitors but not overwhelming.
Peak Semana Santa period in some years; religious processions and local festivities attract many visitors; pleasant spring weather.
Warm spring weather (17-19°C); less crowded than Semana Santa but increasing tourism; ideal for outdoor exploration.
Excellent weather with sunny days and temperatures around 20°C; ideal for beach walks and outdoor dining; moderate crowds.
Consistently pleasant weather; spring flowers in bloom; great for hiking, cultural visits, and early beach days; manageable crowd levels.

ranges.event: Fiesta de la Cruz (May Day Cross Festival)
ranges.notes: Warm spring weather (20-23°C); local festivals and outdoor events; increasing visitors but still comfortable crowds.
Ideal weather with sunny skies and warm temperatures; beaches start to be inviting; perfect for all traveler types.
Consistently warm and sunny; ideal for outdoor excursions, water sports, and cultural events; crowd levels rising but manageable.
Early summer warmth with temperatures around 23°C; perfect for beach lovers and festival goers; moderate to high crowds start.
Warm summer weather with average highs around 27°C; start of high season; lively nightlife and beach activities; higher prices and crowds.
Consistently hot and sunny; beaches crowded but lively; excellent for luxury travelers and couples seeking vibrant atmosphere.

ranges.event: Nit de l'Art (Art Night - mid June, variable date)
ranges.notes: Festival of contemporary art with many exhibitions and events; weather ideal for late evening walks; attracts art lovers and tourists.
Peak summer conditions; warm seas and long daylight hours; busy but lively; best for travelers who enjoy active social scenes.
Hot summer weather (28-30°C); high season with crowded beaches and busy city; vibrant nightlife and many cultural events.
Peak tourist season with warm sea temperatures; beaches crowded; great for water sports and nightlife; prices at peak.
Consistently hot and vibrant; best for travelers who enjoy lively beach and party atmosphere; less ideal for budget travelers and those avoiding crowds.
High season continues with warm nights and active cultural scene; suitable for social travelers and luxury market.
Very hot summer weather (29-31°C); extremely crowded with tourists; some locals leave the city; prices peak; great for beach lovers but crowded.
High temperatures and humidity; crowded beaches and attractions; good for travelers seeking nightlife and beach parties; less ideal for families and budget travelers.

ranges.event: Fiesta de la Mare de Déu d'Agost (Assumption Day celebrations, Aug 15)
ranges.notes: Traditional religious celebrations with local festivals; very crowded but culturally rich; weather hot and sunny.
Late summer heat with crowds starting to slightly decrease after mid-August; still busy and lively with warm sea.
Warm early autumn weather (25-28°C); sea remains warm; crowds begin to thin; ideal for beach and cultural activities.
Pleasant temperatures and lower humidity; fewer tourists; good deals start appearing; excellent for all traveler types.
Stable mild weather with sunny days; great for hiking, sightseeing, and wine festivals starting in Mallorca region.
Slightly cooler evenings; some rain possible; fewer tourists but still good outdoor conditions; ideal for cultural travelers.
Mild autumn weather (20-23°C); reduced crowds; some rainy days but many sunny; good for sightseeing and wine tasting.

ranges.event: Fiesta de Sant Narcís (regional festival near Palma late October)
ranges.notes: Local cultural events and festivals; weather comfortable for outdoor activities; fewer tourists, authentic experience.
Cooler temperatures (17-19°C); increased rainfall; quieter city; some indoor cultural activities recommended.
Increasingly cooler and wetter; off-season for tourism; good for budget travelers and those seeking quiet cultural visits.
Cooler and wetter weather; average highs around 15°C; limited outdoor activities; low tourism activity.
Grey skies and rainy days common; many hotels and restaurants reduce hours; best suited for budget or business travelers.
Off-season with cool weather; limited beach and outdoor options; low prices but less vibrant atmosphere.

ranges.event: All Saints Day (Nov 1) and local remembrances
ranges.notes: Some cultural and religious observances; weather remains cool and rainy; still low tourist activity but cultural interest possible.
Cool weather with occasional rain; festive holiday decorations begin; moderate local events attract visitors.
Winter weather with short days; quieter city; good for cultural visits and Christmas market preparations.
Christmas markets and holiday lights increase atmosphere; cooler but festive; moderate crowds mainly locals and some tourists.

ranges.event: Christmas and New Year's Eve celebrations
ranges.notes: Festive holiday period with special events and celebrations; weather cool but city is lively; higher crowds especially around New Year's Eve.
